tables.name: player_profiles
tables.description: Saved host and participant identity records

tables.name: active_rooms
tables.description: Saved room, player, and lobby/game state

questions.prompt: What is the primary role of a QE (Quality Engineer)?
options: Writing production feature code
Managing cloud infrastructure
Ensuring software meets quality standards through testing and validation
Designing UI mockups in Figma
questions.explanation: QEs are responsible for verifying that software works correctly, meets requirements, and is free of critical defects.

questions.prompt: What does 'Acceptance Criteria' define in a user story?
options: The database schema
The deployment schedule
The conditions that must be met for the story to be considered done
The color palette for the UI
questions.explanation: Acceptance Criteria spell out exactly what 'done' looks like — the testable conditions a feature must satisfy.

options: A new JavaScript framework for animations
Pair programming with background music
Writing code while meditating
Describing intent in natural language and letting AI generate the code
questions.explanation: Vibe coding means you express what you want conversationally and AI handles the implementation — coding by intent, not by keystroke.

questions.prompt: In Spec Driven Development (SDD), what comes first?
options: Writing unit tests
Deploying to production
Writing a detailed specification document before any code
Creating the database tables
questions.explanation: SDD means writing a clear, detailed spec first — then AI agents read that spec and generate production-quality code from it.

questions.prompt: What does MCP (Model Context Protocol) allow AI to do?
options: Run faster on older hardware
Connect to and interact with external tools like Jira, qTest, and Figma
Train itself without human data
Replace the operating system
questions.explanation: MCP is a standard that lets AI agents natively read from and write to external tools — no copy-pasting between screens.

options: Build Deploy Destroy
Bug Driven Debugging
Behavior Driven Development
Binary Data Distribution
questions.explanation: BDD uses Given-When-Then scenarios to describe expected software behavior in plain language before tests are written.

questions.prompt: What is 'test coverage' a measure of?
options: How many developers are on the team
How fast the tests run
How much of the codebase is exercised by tests
How many bugs are in production
questions.explanation: Test coverage tells you what percentage of your code is actually being tested — higher coverage means fewer blind spots.

options: When the GPU overheats
When the AI generates confident but factually incorrect output
When the screen flickers
When the model runs out of memory
questions.explanation: AI hallucination is when the model produces something that sounds right but is actually wrong — a key risk when using AI for test generation.

options: A Wi-Fi signal booster for office buildings
A GPS app for Disney parks
A new web browser
An AI toolset of rules, skills, and agents distributed across IDEs via a CLI
questions.explanation: waiFinder packages AI rules, skills, and agents in one source and distributes them to Cursor, Kiro, GitHub Copilot, and Amazon Q via the wai CLI.

options: A test that only runs on Mondays
A test to verify that new changes haven't broken existing functionality
A test that measures server temperature
A test written by the product owner
questions.explanation: Regression tests re-check existing features after code changes to make sure nothing that was working before is now broken.

questions.prompt: What does 'shift left' mean in quality engineering?
options: Moving the keyboard to the left
Starting testing and quality activities earlier in the development lifecycle
Deploying to the left data center
Reducing the font size in test reports
questions.explanation: Shift left means catching defects earlier — during requirements and design — instead of waiting until after code is written.

options: A pop-up error message
A keyboard shortcut
The instruction or question you give to an AI model
A type of database query
questions.explanation: A prompt is what you type to tell the AI what you want — the quality of your prompt directly affects the quality of the output.

options: Testing software without predefined scripts, using creativity and domain knowledge
Testing only the login page
Running automated tests overnight
Reading the source code without executing it
questions.explanation: Exploratory testing is unscripted, experience-driven testing where the QE uses judgment and curiosity to find defects that scripted tests miss.

options: How fast water drains from the server room
How many bugs escape testing and reach production
How many lines of code were deleted
How long the build takes to compile
questions.explanation: Defect leakage is the percentage of bugs that slip past QE into production — a key quality metric that teams track to improve their testing process.

questions.prompt: Why is SDD (Spec Driven Development) valuable for QE?
options: It eliminates the need for any testing
It gives QE a clear, detailed spec to derive test cases from before code is written
It automatically deploys code to production
It replaces the QE role entirely with AI
questions.explanation: When developers write detailed specs before coding, QE gets a clear contract to test against — no more guessing what the feature is supposed to do.

options: When the GPU overheats
When the AI generates confident but factually incorrect output
When the screen flickers
When the model runs out of memory
questions.explanation: AI hallucination is when the model produces something that sounds right but is actually wrong — a key risk when using AI for test generation.
